entities.name: Acyl-CoA Synthetase Long Chain Family Member 4
cystine transporter solute carrier family 7 member 11
cystine transporter solute carrier family 3 member 2
vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1-like 1

Ferroptosis—The "Double-Edged Sword" in Cancer: Mechanisms of Tumor Suppression/Resistance and Therapeutic Manipulation

evidence_entities.text: Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of programmed cell death that is driven by the accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), leading to the formation of membrane-damaging lipid peroxides
This form of cell death is considered to be distinct from other forms, such as apoptosis, pyroptosis, and necroptosis, in both morphological and mechanistic ways
Dixon et al. were among the first to discuss cell death differences between ferroptosis and other forms, including apoptosis

Iron in its ferrous state (Fe2+) is required for the Fenton reaction, a chemical process in which ferrous iron ions are used in the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to create reactive hydroxyl radicals

Several enzymes are involved in the peroxidation of PUFAs, including Acyl-CoA Synthetase Long Chain Family Member 4 (ACSL4), Lysophosphatidylcholine Acyltransferase 3 (LPCAT3), arachidonate lipoxygenases (ALOXs), and cytochrome P450 oxidoreductase (POR)
TP53 is the most frequently mutated tumor-suppressor gene and can play an important role in the promotion of ferroptosis in cancer
Erastin acts through the inhibition of the xCT antiporter (or Xc− system) composed of both SLC7A11 and SLC3A2
RSL3 is another induction molecule that directly inhibits GPX4 by bypassing the need for GSH depletion

properties.note: SLC7A11 is the gene symbol for cystine transporter solute carrier family 7 member 11
